claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, Blake, and Charlie, I need to ask you some questions about each of them.

Let's start with their ages. How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 4 years old, Blake is 16, and Charlie is 15.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Now I need to ask about where you live and your responsibilities for the children.

Do you live in the UK, and do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester. Alex and Charlie live with me at home, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Blake doesn't live with you, I need to ask: do you contribute financially towards Blake's upkeep? This could include things like food, clothes, pocket money, or contributions towards their accommodation - and it would need to be at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Blake's upkeep. I help with things like clothes, food, and general expenses - I give about £50 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to ask about Blake and Charlie since they're both teenagers.

Is Blake (who is 16) currently in approved education or training? This could include things like school, college, A-levels, or approved training courses.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake actually left school recently, but they've just signed up with the local careers service to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. According to the rules, if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks.

How long ago did Blake leave school and register with the careers service?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been a few weeks now - they registered pretty soon after leaving school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And what about Charlie, who is 15 - is Charlie still in school or approved education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is still in school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Has anyone else claimed Child Benefit for any of these children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect. One final question: do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000? This wouldn't affect your eligibility, but it may mean you'd need to pay a tax charge called the High Income Child Benefit charge.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'd rather not get into my income details right now - I'm mainly just trying to find out if I'm eligible for the benefit itself.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's absolutely fine - the income question doesn't affect your eligibility, just whether there might be a tax charge later on, so we can proceed with the eligibility assessment.

Based on everything you've told me, let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your children:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Good News! You Are Eligible for Child Benefit

You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for **all three of your children**: Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Here's the breakdown:

**Alex (age 4):** ✓ Eligible
- Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you're responsible for them. You meet all the standard criteria.

**Blake (age 16):** ✓ Eligible  
- Even though Blake doesn't live with you, you're contributing around £50 per week towards their upkeep, which is at least equivalent to Child Benefit payments. Additionally, since Blake recently left school and registered with the careers service, you can claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks from when they registered. You're currently within that timeframe.

**Charlie (age 15):** ✓ Eligible
- Charlie is under 16, still in school, lives with you in the UK, and you're responsible for them. You meet all the standard criteria.

### Important Note for Blake:
The 20-week period for Blake (since registering with the careers service) is temporary. You'll want to monitor Blake's situation - if they start approved education, training, or work within that period, it may affect continued eligibility beyond the 20 weeks.

Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of your children, you're free to make your claim. Would you like information on how to claim?

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You are responsible for Alex as they live with you. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you, but you are contributing towards their upkeep (approximately £50 per week for clothes, food, and general expenses), which is at least equivalent to Child Benefit payments. Blake has recently left school and registered with the local careers service. Under the rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks when a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Since it has been a few weeks since Blake registered, you are still within this 20-week period. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. You meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 15 years old (under 16), is still in school (approved education), lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with three children: Alex (4), Blake (16), and Charlie (15). Alex and Charlie both live with the claimant, are under 16/in approved education, and meet standard eligibility criteria. Blake does not live with the claimant but the claimant contributes approximately £50 weekly towards Blake's upkeep. Blake recently left school and registered with the careers service, which allows Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of the children. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for all three children.

Charlie is 15 years old (under 16), is still in school (approved education), lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.
